topic: Two friends both apply for the same job
raw_output:
[story 1]

Elias stared at the crumpled application form on his desk. The ink seemed to swim under the harsh fluorescent light. He kept running a finger over the section where he’d meticulously tailored his experience, a familiar, cold knot tightening in his chest. When he remembered Leo mentioning he was applying for the exact same opening, a strange hollowness washed over him. He stood up abruptly, pacing the length of his small apartment, his breath coming in short, shallow gasps. Every time he glanced at his phone, he half-expected a notification—a confirmation, a rejection—anything to break the paralyzing quiet that had settled over him. He picked up a mug, the ceramic suddenly feeling heavy and cold in his grip, and forced himself to look at the interview schedule, the paper blurring slightly at the edges.

[story 2]

"Are you sure about this, Maya?" Ben asked, his voice tight, leaning against the doorway of the coffee shop. He watched her meticulously smooth the edges of her resume for the third time. A tremor ran through her hand as she placed the paper down. She swallowed hard, her throat suddenly dry. The air in the small waiting room felt thick, pressurized. She found herself tapping her foot rapidly against the linoleum floor, a nervous rhythm she couldn't control. *It can’t be,* she thought, the conviction warring with a sudden, sharp doubt. She looked at the clock ticking on the wall, the seconds stretching into an unbearable expanse. She smoothed her skirt, a purely reflexive action, trying to anchor herself against the rising tide of anxiety churning beneath her ribs.

[story 3]

Sarah reread the job description, the words echoing hollowly in her mind. She chewed on the end of her pen, the plastic tasting metallic on her tongue. When she finally looked up, her gaze drifted to the framed photo on her wall—a picture of her and Alex from college. The familiar warmth of the memory offered no comfort; instead, it amplified the strange tension coiling in her stomach. She began fiddling with a loose thread on her sleeve, pulling at it repeatedly, her focus fractured. Every sound in the office—the distant phone ringing, the shuffling of papers—seemed amplified, drawing her attention to the sheer improbability of the situation. She shifted in her chair, trying to find a position that didn't feel quite right, a physical manifestation of the internal disquiet she couldn't quite name.
